course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not for students enrolled in LLB(Hons)(#64), LLB(Hons)(#48), LLB, LLB (Grad Entry), LLB(Hons) duals and LLB dual programs
requirements.assumed_background: The taxation of business income and passive income (for example dividends and rental income) differs, depending on the legal structures used to conduct the business and the ownership rights of the entities that own the investments that give rise to the passive income. The most common legal entities are sole traders, partnerships, companies and discretionary trusts. Students are expected to be familiar with the ownership rights associated with sole traders, partnerships and companies from their studies of LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent).

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Lectures

Lectures are timetabled and conducted in person on campus. A recording of each lecture will be made available in Learning Resources on the course Blackboard site. Students are not required to preference a lecture in My Timetable. Students are expected to attend or watch the weekly lectures in the week when each lecture takes place, as the Tutorial Program relies on students' knowledge gained from the previous week’s lecture.

Tutorials

Students are required to log their preference for a tutorial group via My Timetable (available through my.UQ dashboard) so that they choose a tutorial group that fits in with their personal circumstance . Students are required to enrol in a tutorial group and to attend tutorials in the tutorial group they are enrolled in according to their personal timetable . Enrolment in each tutorial group reflects the small-group interactive approach followed in the Tutorial Program that facilitates and encourages active student participation. It is not possible to attend another tutorial group, as summative tutorial assessment is linked to the tutorial group each student is enrolled in on their own personal timetable. It is therefore very important that each student proactively preferences a tutorial group that suits their personal circumstances . See the Learning Activities section of this Course Profile (Section 4.1) for timetabling implications of public holidays.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims:

The aim of LAWS3101 is to equip you with the skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify the sources of taxation law and the regulatory framework in which taxation is administered in Australia.
Identify and apply the sources of taxation law to solve real-world income tax problems.
Construct and explain the calculation of taxable income and taxes payable.
Calculate and describe the appropriate Goods and Services Tax treatment of transactions.
Identify, categorise and calculate the Fringe Benefits Tax consequences of employee entitlements.

assessment_summary.title: Solving real-world tax problems
assessment_summary.due_date: In-class, specific window in each student's own timetabled tutorial class or per TUT instructions

assessment_details.title: Solving real-world tax problems
assessment_details.due_date: In-class, specific window in each student's own timetabled tutorial class or per TUT instructions
assessment_details.task_description:
Tutorials provide students with guided opportunities to practice their skills to solve real-world problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in-class . Students are expected to prepare for and attend their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class they are enrolled in each week. Students are expected to participate in their tutorial class so that they are able to complete a number of tutorial exercises each week that will enable them to complete and submit summative tutorial assessments in-class during the specific short time window period in their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class as instructed by their tutor. Students must be present for the duration of their tutorial class to be eligible to complete their summative tutorial assessments online in-class during the specific short time window period in their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class as instructed by their tutor. For purposes of Tutorial 8 only, students must submit their answers online in Word on the course site before 11 am on Monday 9 October 2023. To be eligible to have their summative assessment tasks graded:

Students must attend each respective tutorial class for the duration of each tutorial class according to their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class; and

Students signed on to face-to-face tutorials must write their name and surname on the weekly attendance roll and must sign their name on the weekly attendance roll; and

Students signed on to online tutorials must reveal their name and surname on their video in zoom and must have their cameras turned on for the duration of each tutorial class so that they can be identified during the tutorial class and observed for purposes of the in-class assessment; and

Students must complete their on-line assessment in Tutorial 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9 during the specific short time window period when instructed to do so by their tutors.

For flexibility, there are eight opportunities to complete and submit summative tutorial assessments tasks. Each summative tutorial assessment counts a maximum of 5%. The total maximum mark for tutorial exercises is 30%. Each student’s total mark for tutorials, capped at 30%, are taken into account in calculating their final mark for LAWS3101.

Format of summative tutorial assessments

Summative tutorial assessments in Tutorial 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9 are based on the tutorial exercises that students prepare for and complete in-class. Tutorial 8 requires students to submit answers in Word online before 11 am on Monday 9 October 2023. Summative tutorial assessments are completed online in Blackboard. Summative tutorial assessments may include: multiple choice questions, short answer responses, true/false questions, document uploads, short essays, reflective writing and completing marking rubrics.

Due dates and times

Each student must attend their own timetabled scheduled tutorial to complete and submit their summative tutorial assessment tasks for Tutorial 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 9 online in-class during the specific short time window period indicated by their tutor during their weekly 50-minute tutorial class scheduled according to each student’s personal timetable.

The in-class submission time of each summative tutorial assessment varies between students and is governed by each student’s individual timetabled scheduled tutorial class reflected in their personal timetable and is governed by the specific time window that tutors instruct students to commence their assessment during each tutorial class. In accordance with these individual times for the window period in each student’s personal timetable:

Tutorial 1 summative assessment tasks are due in class in Teaching Week 2.

Tutorial 2 summative assessment tasks are due in-class in Teaching Week 3.

Tutorial 3 summative assessment tasks are due in-class in Teaching Week 5.

Tutorial 4 summative assessment tasks are due in-class in Teaching Week 6.

Tutorial 5 summative assessment tasks are due in-class in Teaching Week 7.

Tutorial 6 summative assessment tasks are due in-class in Teaching Week 9.

Tutorial 8 summative assessment tasks are due before 11 am on Monday 9 October 2023.

Tutorial 9 summative assessment tasks are due in-class in Teaching Week 12.

This assessment task evaluates student’s abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Tutorials are individual items of assessments. Students must work alone.

assessment_details.title: Mid Semester Exam (online timed exam)
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid Semester Exam covers Topic 1, Topic 2, Topic 3, Topic 4 and Topic 5. The Mid Semester Exam is a timed non-invigilated online exam that starts at 12:15 pm and ends at 1:15 pm (Brisbane time zone) that includes 10 minutes of planning time. This is an individual assessment activity. Students must work alone. The Mid Semester Exam is an open book exam. However, as this is a timed exam, students are expected to know all of the work covered in the exam and the Mid Semester Exam is set on that basis. Therefore, in setting the exam, no time allowance is made for students to look up the answers. Students are not required to follow the ILAC approach in answering questions (Identify the issue - State the law - Apply the law to the facts - Conclude). Students must answer all of the questions in the exam in an online test tool. All students start the Mid Semester Exam at the same time.

This assessment task evaluates student’s abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessment_details.title: Online Final Examination - Invigilated
assessment_details.format: Short answer, Short essay, Extended essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The exam will be an open book (only printed/written materials are permitted) online Blackboard exam, invigilated through ProctorU . Access to the internet or files on the computer is not permitted. However, as this is a timed exam, students are expected to know all of the work covered in the exam and the Final Exam is set on that basis. Therefore, in setting the exam, no time allowance is made for students to look up the answers.

The Final Exam covers Topic 3, Topic 4, Topic 5, Topic 6, Topic 7, Topic 8, Topic 9 and Topic 10.

The Final Exam consists of a number of questions that may include short answer questions, short essay questions, practical calculation questions, discussion questions and problem solving questions. Students are not required to follow the ILAC approach in answering questions (Identify the issue - State the law - Apply the law to the facts - Conclude).

You must use a UQ-approved calculator to sit your exam.

In the event you do not have a valid UQ student ID card, a student may produce a government issued identification document.

Have your UQ student ID card available for all of your exams. Please ensure that you use the most reliable internet connection that you have access to when you are completing the exam. If you use an insecure connection, you may lose connectivity and lose some or all of the work you have completed. Please make sure that you keep copies of the receipts that you receive when you have completed and submitted the exam.

Students will not be permitted to change their exam mode unless there are exceptional circumstances supported by documentary evidence and approved by Examinations.

learning_resources.required_resources:
Students must have their own copy of: Principles of Taxation Law 2023 Edition . Thomson Reuters. K Sadiq (General Editor). Paper copy ISBN 9780455246895. E-book ISBN 9780455246901. ​Permitted materials Final Exam, printed (paper) materials only. If you purchase the E-book, you must print the pages you wish to use during the Final Exam.

Topic Learning Guides. Individual Topic Learning Guides will be released progressively to the LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) weekly. Students will find these in the weekly folders in Learning resources.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
The following acts will be referenced during the course:

Income Tax Assessment Act 1997

Income tax Assessment Act 1936

A New Tax System (GST) Act 1999

Fringe Benefits Tax Assessment Act 1986.

These are available online:

Australian Legal Information Institute, www.austlii.edu.au

ATO, www.ato.gov.au

The relevant parts of the aforementioned legislation will be included in the learning guides for the relevant topics if students are required to study these. Students will not be required to search these online resources to prepare for assessments.

Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website, incorporating the ATO Legal Database helpful.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Which resources will help you to meet the assessment criteria of LAWS3101?

Taxation law in Australia is rules-based. To meet the assessment criteria of LAWS3101, your answers must include specific sections and divisions, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings that are listed in the topic Learning guides. It is essential to consult each topic’s Learning guide to know:

Which specific material from Principles of Taxation Law 2023 you are required to study for each topic.

Which specific sections and divisions from statute law and which ATO Rulings numbers to include in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria.

Which specific case law citations to include in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria.

Where can you find more questions and model answers for each topic?

Each topic Learning guide will include a series of questions and model answers to help you to understand the material covered in each topic and how to meet the assessment criteria.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

Tutorial classes provide students with a guided opportunity each week to practice their skills to solve real-world tax problems and to receive feedback. To maximise the benefits of tutorials, students are expected to prepare for and attend all of their own timetabled scheduled tutorial classes and to participate.

Each topic learning guide includes a series of questions and answers to help students evaluate whether they can effectively meet the specific assessment criteria in LAWS3101.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://law.uq.edu.au/files/7645/TL14-Feedback-on-final-examinations.pdf ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 6 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
LAWS3101 is an introductory course in taxation law that covers income taxes levied on the taxable income of a taxpayer (including Capital Gains Tax), Goods and Service Tax (GST), and Fringe Benefits Tax (FBT). LAWS3101 will set you on a path so that you are able to think about the real-world implications of taxation.

Taxation law is complex, even at an introductory level , and a continually changing area of the law. Your biggest challenge in the course is to navigate the rules-based nature of taxation law which is unlike the principles-based nature of accounting and finance courses. Each LAWS3101 topic covers specific sections and divisions of statute law, specific case law and specific Australian Taxation Office (ATO) Rulings that are essential elements of your answers in assessments in order to meet the assessment criteria of the course. LAWS3101 will equip you with the skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings.

Accreditation

LAWS3101 is accredited by Chartered Accountants Australia New Zealand (CAANZ) and CPA Australia. The scope and depth of topics covered, and assessment in the course, aligns to accreditation.

Janine McMaster-Kirkwood Prize in Income Tax Law

The student with the best performance in their first attempt at LAWS3101 will be recommended for this prize. One prize will be awarded. This prize is not shared between students.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The Tutorial Program has been adapted so that students are able to practice their skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in-class. Students will be expected to attend their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class and to complete a number of tutorial exercises that will enable them to complete and submit summative tutorial assessment tasks in-class during their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class . For this reason, it is of the utmost importance that students preference a tutorial group that fits their personal circumstances.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
VERY IMPORTANT - Please read.

Your weekly time-investment in LAWS3101

Taxation law is complex, even at an introductory level. To achieve your GPA goals, you must be prepared to make a serious 8-hour time commitment to your studies of LAWS3101 each week.

Due to accreditation, the volume and depth of work covered in LAWS3101 is significantly more than in most of your other courses. To navigate this volume and complexity, you must expect to devote more time to LAWS3101 than you might be accustomed to in other courses. You must use the topic learning guides to navigate the coursework.

Taxation law in Australia is rules-based. This means that you must know, apply and include the following in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria: specific section numbers, specific division numbers, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings numbers stipulated in the topic learning guides to demonstrate that you know how to solve real-world tax problems. This level of detail is very different to the principles-based nature of accounting and finance courses. Plan to adapt your usual study technique to make the best use of your 8-hour time investment in LAWS3101 each week.

To help you plan your learning activities, we provide you with a daily diary in the weekly folders in Learning Resources on Blackboard that directs you to a range of course resources. Your most important learning activity each week is to study the topic materials from Principles of Taxation Law 2023 (PoTL). To know which materials to study, you must consult the topic learning guides in the weekly folders in Learning Resources on Blackboard. Plan to study the topic materials from PoTL. It is not an effective study technique to read these materials . Be prepared to undertake significant self-directed learning from PoTL each week.

Lectures

Lectures are timetabled and conducted in-person. You should attend the weekly lectures because the lecture content is assumed knowledge for and forms the basis of the next week’s tutorial exercises. If you are unable to attend the in-person lecture, you should watch the recording that will be released to Blackboard in that week, to avoid falling behind.

During lectures we introduce you to each topic and discuss more complex aspects of each topic in preparation for your tutorials in each following week. Because taxation law is rules-based, it is not possible to include even the majority of section and division numbers, case law and ATO Rulings that you must know and apply in lecture materials. The purpose of lectures is not to share all the course materials with you. Your primary source of the course materials is PoTL as directed in the topic learning guides.

Tutorials

The Tutorial Program provides you with guided opportunities to practice your skills to solve real-world tax problems using statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in-class . Enrolment in each tutorial group reflects the small-group interactive approach followed in the Tutorial Program that facilitates and encourages active student participation. It is not possible to attend another tutorial group as summative tutorial assessment is linked to the tutorial group each student is enrolled in according to their personal timetable. It is your responsibility to proactively preference for a tutorial group via My Timetable (available through my.UQ dashboard) so that you choose a tutorial group that fits in with your personal circumstance.

You will be expected to prepare for each tutorial class by attending/watching the previous week’s lecture and reading the tutorial materials provided to you.

You will be expected to complete all of the tutorial preparation tasks as set out in each tutorial document.

You will be expected to attend all your tutorial classes.

You must attend your own timetabled scheduled tutorial class.

Tutorials are not recorded as students are expected to prepare for and attend their timetabled scheduled tutorial classes and to actively participate to complete the tutorial exercises so that they are equipped to complete and submit their summative tutorial assessment tasks.

To support student learning, tutorial feedback will be released from time-to-time.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +
late_submission:
Penalties for Late Submission

Late submission of summative tutorial assessment tasks is not possible .

For purposes of Tutorial 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9, students must attend their own timetabled scheduled tutorial class to complete and submit their summative tutorial assessment tasks in-class during the specific short time window period indicated by their tutor in their own timetabled scheduled tutorial. At the end of each specific short time window period during each student's own timetabled scheduled tutorial class, their online tutorial assessment will close and submit automatically.

For purposes of Tutorial 8, students must submit their answers online before 11 am on Monday 9 October 2023.

Extension to Assessment Due Date

Extensions will not normally exceed 14 days. If you have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 14 days, we advise you to consider carefully whether you are capable of successfully completing your courses this semester. You might be eligible to withdraw without academic penalty.

Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension. Students must apply for an extension online.

Reasons for extension and evidence :

• If the basis of the application is a medical condition:

o Applications must be accompanied by a medical certificate dated on or before the assignment due date.

o It is your responsibility to ensure that the provider of your medical certificate is a genuine medical practitioner. You will be held fully responsible for whatever you submit, even if you do so in apparent ignorance. It is therefore your responsibility to ensure that anything you submit is authentic.

o An extension granted to a student on medical grounds will not exceed the number of days suffered from a medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate. The medical condition must have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment.

• If the basis of the application is some other circumstance:

o The circumstances must have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

o the extension granted will be in proportion to the period of disruption caused by the circumstances, and be no more than two weeks in duration.

Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply .

Applications to be submitted before the due date : Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment . Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date will not be accepted unless there is evidence to demonstrate extenuating circumstances that made it impossible to submit the request earlier. Late requests received by the School will require the decision maker listed in section 2.3.2 of PPL 3.60.02 Student Grievance Resolution to accept the request for consideration.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.
